title: Choosing meat rabbit breeds for the UK
subtitle: Growth rate, litter size, temperament, and the practical differences between the main options.
excerpt: New Zealand White, Californian, Flemish Giant, or a commercial cross: the breed you choose for a meat rabbit system affects how fast kits grow, how many the doe weans per litter, and how easy the animals are to handle. This tutorial compares the main options available to small-scale UK producers.
sourceNotes: British Rabbit Council breed standards. Cornell Small Farms, Rabbit Production for Small Farms bulletin. Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, rabbit chapters.

glossaryTerms.definition: The dominant commercial meat rabbit breed worldwide. Fast-maturing, reaching 2 kg liveweight at 8-10 weeks. Large litters (8-12 kits average). White coat and pink eyes; calm temperament. The reference breed for UK commercial production.
A newborn or young rabbit from birth until weaning at approximately 8 weeks. Born blind, deaf, and hairless; depend entirely on the doe for warmth and milk for the first 21 days.

An adult female rabbit. A doe enters her first productive breeding cycle at 5-6 months for small breeds and 7-8 months for large breeds. A well-managed meat doe produces 6-8 litters per year in intensive systems; 4-5 litters per year is a more sustainable rate for a smallholding.

content.text: The choice of meat rabbit breed affects the economics and practicality of the whole system. The two variables that matter most are daily weight gain (how fast a
reaches slaughter weight) and litter size (how many kits a
consistently produces per litter). Beyond that, temperament and heat tolerance matter for a small farm setting.

is the standard commercial meat breed. It grows to 2 kg liveweight at 8-10 weeks (slaughter weight for fryers) and to 2.5-3 kg at 12 weeks (roaster weight). Litter sizes of 8-12 are reliable. The breed is calm, easy to handle, and tolerates a range of housing conditions. The pink eyes are an albino trait from the lack of pigment; they are functional.

The Californian (white body with dark nose, ears, feet, and tail) is the other standard commercial breed. Similar growth rate to New Zealand White, slightly smaller frame. Often crossed with NZW for commercial production; the hybrid typically outperforms either purebred parent in litter size and growth uniformity. A Californian doe crossed with an NZW buck is a common starting point for a small production system.

The Flemish Giant is the largest domestic rabbit breed. Adults reach 6-9 kg liveweight. The appeal is the size; the practical problem is the growth rate. Flemish Giants grow slowly and reach slaughter weight at 16-20 weeks rather than 8-10, consuming far more feed per kg of meat produced. They suit a small-scale, high-cost production system where the size of the finished rabbit is the selling point; they are not the right choice for efficiency-focused production.

Rex rabbits (plush coat) and Satins (lustrous coat) grow more slowly and have smaller litters than the commercial breeds, but are dual-purpose options where the pelt has value alongside the carcass. NZW crosses with Rex produce a practical meat rabbit, calmer than the purebred NZW, with a usable pelt.
For a beginner system of 20-40 rabbits per year, the NZW x Californian cross is the best UK starting point. It grows fast, weans large litters, and is easy to handle without specialist breeding.
